era_context: The early-to-mid 14th century: the Hundred Years' War is beginning (1337); the Black Death is spreading along trade routes; the Yuan Dynasty is fragmenting; the Mamluk Sultanate is at its height; and European feudalism is under structural stress.

events.title: Battle of Sluys — English naval victory over France
events.description: On 24 June 1340, an English fleet under Edward III defeated a French fleet at Sluys (in present-day Belgium), near the mouth of the Zwin River. The English destroyed or captured most of the French ships, killing thousands of French sailors. This naval victory gave England control of the English Channel and was a major early success in the Hundred Years' War. The battle demonstrated English naval superiority and Edward III's military competence. However, the victory did not lead to immediate territorial gains on land.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Sluys is well-documented in contemporary English and French sources.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Edward III's claim to French throne
events.description: In 1340, Edward III formally claimed the French throne, asserting his right through his mother Isabella of France. This claim was the legal and dynastic basis for the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453). Edward III's claim was contested by the Valois dynasty (Philip VI and his successors), leading to over a century of intermittent warfare. In 1340, Edward III was consolidating his position as a major European power.
events.certainty_note: Edward III's claim to the French throne is well-documented.

events.title: Black Death spreads in Asia; plague in Central Asia and China
events.description: The bubonic plague (Yersinia pestis) was spreading along Silk Road trade routes in 1340. Chinese sources document plague deaths in Central Asia and possibly in China during this period. The exact chronology is debated, but by 1340, the pandemic was well-established in Asia. The disease would reach the Mediterranean by 1347, devastating Europe.

sources.name: Yuan Shi and Chinese local records

events.certainty_note: Plague's presence in Asia by 1340 is supported by Chinese sources, though exact dating varies.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun; internal conflict and famine
events.description: In 1340, the Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun (Toghon Temür, r. 1333–1368) was experiencing severe internal conflict and famine. Factional struggles among Mongol nobility, Han Chinese officials, and regional warlords were destabilizing the empire. Peasant unrest was growing. The empire would ultimately collapse to the Ming Dynasty (1368). In 1340, the Yuan was still nominally powerful but increasingly fragile.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Shun of Yuan, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan, compiled by Ming historians)
Chinese administrative documents and local records

events.certainty_note: Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented; specific 1340 events are less detailed.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad; Eastern Mediterranean dominance
events.description: In 1340, the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt under al-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341) was at the height of its power. Al-Nasir Muhammad had consolidated Mamluk control over Egypt, the Levant, and the Hijaz (including the holy cities of Mecca and Medina). He was known for his military strength, his patronage of architecture and Islamic learning, and his diplomatic relations with European and Asian powers. In 1340, al-Nasir Muhammad was in his final year of rule.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (History of the Mamluks, written early 15th century)
Contemporary Islamic chronicles and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign is well-documented in Islamic sources.

events.title: Mali Empire under Mansa Maghan I; West African trade networks
events.description: In 1340, Mali was ruled by Mansa Maghan I (r. c. 1337–1341 or c. 1341–1360, dates disputed). Mali was a major power in West African gold trade and Islamic scholarship. The empire controlled trans-Saharan trade routes and maintained diplomatic relations with North African and Middle Eastern powers. In 1340, Mali's influence was still strong, though the empire was beginning to face competition from rising regional powers.
key_figures: Mansa Maghan I (r. c. 1337–1341 or c. 1341–1360, dates disputed)

sources.name: Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash (West African chronicles)

events.certainty_note: Mali's political history in the 1340s relies on oral tradition and later chronicles. Succession dates are disputed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Sluys is sometimes overstated in importance; while it was a significant naval victory, it did not lead to immediate territorial gains or a decisive English advantage in the Hundred Years' War. Edward III's claim to the French throne, while legally asserted in 1340, was not universally recognized and remained contested throughout the war. Mali's rulers in the 1340s are often conflated or misnamed; the exact succession and dates are disputed. The plague's spread in 1340 is less well-documented than in 1344–1347; some scholars argue the pandemic's major impact in Asia occurred slightly later.
historiographic_note: 1340 is a year of military and political significance in Europe, but less dramatic in Asia and Africa. European sources (English and French chronicles) are relatively abundant and focus on the Hundred Years' War. Chinese sources document Yuan instability. Islamic sources document the Mamluk Sultanate. West African history relies on oral tradition and later chronicles, with significant gaps. The plague's spread is documented retrospectively. Bias toward European, East Asian, and Islamic history is evident; African and Southeast Asian developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Sluys in 1340 gave England naval superiority, contributing to later victories like Crécy in 1346
Edward III's claim to the French throne in 1340 was the basis for the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453)
Plague spread in Asia in 1340 reached the Mediterranean in 1347
Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign in 1340 ended with his death in 1341
